title: Apple and Audi alumni have made a luxe EV based on the moon buggy
gist: Amble, a Lisbon-based startup founded by alumni of Audi, Apple's Project Titan, and Cowboy ebikes, is launching the $25,000 Amble One — a NASA moon-buggy-inspired L7e street-legal electric buggy targeting luxury hospitality and affluent second-car buyers in Europe and the US, with 500+ units pre-committed and deliveries starting mid-2027. The founders deliberately bypass the saturated urban micromobility market to build premium brand equity first, then expand downmarket. A follow-on 'Amble Two' platform targeting the sub-$50k family second-car slot is planned for 2029.

insights.claim: Amble is targeting luxury hospitality venues (e.g., Amangiri, Mustique Island, Six Senses) as its beachhead market before pursuing consumer sales, aiming to build premium brand credibility before competing in the broader urban micromobility space where earlier entrants failed.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Luxury-channel-first go-to-market sidesteps the price-war dynamic that killed earlier micromobility startups and creates aspirational brand pull before mass-market expansion.
insights.quote: A lot of companies in micromobility start in the urban market and want to compete with everyone, and we all know that this did not work out so far. We're taking a different approach: build our brand as a premium brand, and then step by step go more into this urban market.

insights.claim: Amble has secured over 500 vehicles committed and more than 10 million euros in signed revenue ahead of its stealth-mode exit, providing genuine demand validation rather than pre-order speculation.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: 10M+ euros in signed contracts from hospitality clients gives investors a real revenue baseline and de-risks the launch phase compared to typical EV startups at announcement.
insights.quote: The company apparently has 12 signed clients, over 500 vehicles committed, and more than 10 million euros in signed revenue, according to Roose.

insights.claim: Amble's CEO Adrien Roose explicitly frames the second-car market — not the primary family vehicle — as the addressable opportunity, positioning affordable sub-$50k purpose-built EVs against expensive mainstream EVs like BYD and Tesla models.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: The sub-$50k second-car EV segment is under-served by major OEMs focused on primary vehicle replacement — a structural gap that niche startups could exploit profitably.
insights.quote: Most families do not need twice that $50,000 BYD or Tesla. The second vehicle for families could be something that is designed for purpose, designed for shorter trips—and that can be much simpler, way more fun, way more open, and also more affordable.

themes.theme: L7e regulatory category as competitive moat in Europe

insights.claim: Qualifying as an L7e vehicle (sub-450 kg) in Europe allows road-legal status without full automotive regulatory burden, but the weight constraint is technically demanding enough to screen out incumbents that simply shrink existing car platforms.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: L7e classification creates a regulatory arbitrage window for lightweight EV specialists; conventional OEMs cannot easily enter by downsizing existing architectures.
insights.quote: To qualify as an L7e vehicle in Europe (the category that allows it to drive on public roads without being treated as a car), the Amble One must stay under 450 kilograms. "This is really hard," says Adrien Roose, CEO and cofounder. "If you take a car and just shrink it, it doesn't work."

insights.claim: The Amble One's open, doorless design is a functional engineering choice driven by the 450 kg L7e weight cap, not purely aesthetics — demonstrating how regulatory constraints can define product form factors in micromobility.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Regulatory weight limits will structurally define which design archetypes can compete in the European L7e segment, favoring open or ultra-light body constructions.
insights.quote: The Amble One's open, doorless design isn't just an aesthetic choice that apes competitors such as the electric Moke, it seems. It's part of what helps make the weight target achievable.

themes.theme: Apple Project Titan design talent dispersing into EV startups

insights.claim: Julian Hoenig, who worked on Apple's canceled Project Titan car program as well as the Apple Watch and Vision Pro, has joined Amble as design lead, illustrating how Apple's decade-long automotive R&D investment is now diffusing into the broader EV ecosystem via alumni.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Apple's cancelled car project is functioning as an inadvertent talent incubator; startups that recruit Titan alumni gain differentiated industrial design capabilities at startup scale.
insights.quote: Hoenig spent years at Audi, working on the RSQ, A4, R8, and Q3, then joined Apple's design team, where he worked on the Apple Watch, Vision Pro, and Apple's Project Titan car program.

insights.claim: The design philosophy carried over from Apple's Project Titan to Amble is material-function alignment ('pick the material suited to the job, let manufacturing drive the form'), not specific vehicle technology — suggesting Titan's main transferable legacy is process discipline rather than IP.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Apple's design methodology, not its automotive IP, is the scarce resource flowing into EV startups via Titan alumni — a soft-moat advantage that cannot be replicated by copying hardware specs.
insights.quote: No, nothing from Apple's canceled Project Titan crept into this vehicle, Hoenig says. What did carry over, however, was a philosophy: Pick the material suited to the job, let manufacturing drive the form.

themes.theme: Supermini EV category growth signal from Stellantis and Citroen Ami

insights.claim: Stellantis has announced plans to expand manufacturing capacity for supermini electric cars including the Citroen Ami, signaling that major OEMs are treating the L7e/supermini EV segment as a meaningful growth category rather than a niche experiment.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Stellantis capacity expansion in superminis validates demand for sub-car EVs, creating a rising-tide tailwind for premium entrants like Amble while also signaling intensifying competition.
insights.quote: Stellantis, which owns Citroen, recently announced plans to expand capacity for its supermini electric cars. "This is the beginning of a turning point," Roose says.

insights.claim: The Citroen Ami (28 mph, 46-mile range) is the benchmark competitor for the Amble One, but Amble targets a premium price point ($25,000 vs. the Ami's sub-$10,000 price), differentiating on design pedigree and material quality rather than on cost.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Amble is effectively creating a luxury tier above the Ami in the L7e category; whether premium pricing is sustainable depends on brand execution rather than technical differentiation.
insights.quote: The 28-mph Citroen Ami with its 46-mile range is a prime example.

themes.theme: Amble One product specifications and platform roadmap

insights.claim: The Amble One's core specs — 60+ mile range, 40 mph top speed, sub-450 kg curb weight, 5-hour charge from a standard home socket — position it as a practical daily mobility tool for resort/estate use cases rather than a limited-range novelty.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Sufficient range and charge-speed specs for hospitality deployments remove the key operational friction that hampered earlier electric golf-cart alternatives.
insights.quote: a range of more than 60 miles, a top speed capped at 40 mph, a five-hour charge from any standard home socket, and a curb weight under 450 kilograms (992 pounds)

insights.claim: Amble's 'Amble Two' platform, targeting a 2029 release, is designed to serve as a family's second car with removable doors, lower roofline, and hardtop — a deliberate step toward conventional-car territory while remaining purpose-built for short trips.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: The Amble Two represents the company's longer-term revenue upside; a 2029 timeline gives investors a 3-year window to assess whether the One's hospitality brand-building translates into consumer demand.
insights.quote: A second platform—already in design and targeting a 2029 release—will move further toward conventional-car territory, with removable doors, a lower roofline, and a hardtop; it cleverly aims to replace not a family's primary car, but its second car.

insights.claim: Amble One first hospitality deliveries are scheduled for mid-2027, with European and US consumer deliveries in 2028, giving the company a 12-18 month hospitality-only window to refine the product before consumer scrutiny.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: The hospitality-first delivery sequencing reduces launch risk by providing controlled real-world feedback before mass consumer rollout.
insights.quote: The first hospitality deliveries of the Amble One begin in mid-2027, while consumer preorders for Europe and the US are now open, with deliveries in 2028, starting from $25,000.

themes.theme: Design inspiration: NASA Lunar Roving Vehicle as product template

insights.claim: Hoenig deliberately exposed the Amble One's electric skateboard platform as a design feature rather than hiding it under bodywork, directly mirroring the NASA Lunar Roving Vehicle's visible chassis architecture — a choice that reduces manufacturing complexity and weight.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Visible-skateboard architecture is a design-for-manufacturing choice that simultaneously lowers weight, reduces part count, and creates a distinctive visual identity — a multi-benefit trade-off worth watching in EV platform design broadly.
insights.quote: just as with NASA's $38 million Lunar Roving Vehicle, the electric platform itself is deliberately visible rather than hidden beneath bodywork. "You see the skateboard," Hoenig says. "And then we put toppings on it."

insights.claim: Amble used natural materials (aluminum, leather, cotton, cork) and standardized motorcycle handlebar diameters for accessory mounts, embedding an open accessory ecosystem into the vehicle's hardware from launch.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: An open accessory ecosystem modeled on motorcycle standards could unlock third-party add-on revenue and community engagement, but depends on sufficient unit volume to attract developers.
insights.quote: An interior dashboard bar is deliberately the same diameter as motorcycle handlebars, so any standard bike accessory mounts directly, should you wish to affix your phone.

themes.theme: Founding team pedigree and network advantages

insights.claim: Amble's founding team spans Cowboy ebikes (Adrien Roose), Audi vehicle programs (Julian Hoenig), Forpeople creative agency (Michael Tropper), and São Lourenço do Barrocal luxury estate (José António Uva as chairman) — combining hardware, design, branding, and luxury hospitality network in one team.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: The hospitality network embedded in the founding team (Uva's estate, Tropper's InterContinental Hotels client) directly explains the rapid conversion of 12 signed hospitality clients, suggesting sales cycles will be faster than typical B2B hardware.
insights.quote: Michael Tropper cofounded Forpeople, a 120-person creative agency whose clients include InterContinental Hotels.

insights.claim: Cowboy, the premium electric bike brand Adrien Roose cofounded, demonstrated that consumers will pay a significant premium over commodity e-bikes for design-led micromobility — a proof-of-concept Roose is now applying to four-wheeled L7e EVs.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Roose's Cowboy track record provides evidence that design premium in micromobility is achievable; Amble's $25,000 Amble One pricing test will reveal if the same dynamic holds for four-wheel platforms.
insights.quote: Roose cofounded Cowboy, one of the more recognized premium electric bike brands.
